title: Higher orders with Soroban and Suanpan (算盤)
header: Higher orders with Soroban and Suanpan (算盤)

text.text: ().

### Prerequisites

This text shows how to represent larger numerical orders on the Soroban and Suanpan (算盤) abacuses. For a better understanding, we suggest reading the previous texts on numerical orders (<htcite0>; <htcite1>; <htcite2>; <htcite3>) and the texts that explain how to use the abacus to represent numbers between 0 and 9 (<htcite4>; <htcite5>).

### Introduction

So far, we have seen that, in the Indo-Arabic numerals we use, the numerical order (or families, as we mentioned in the *History Tracers*) ) grows as we write numbers to the left (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

When observing the indigenous numbers of the Americas (*Abya Yala*), we notice that the numerical orders increase vertically (<htcite2>; <htcite3>). It is worth remembering that, in the Americas (*Abya Yala*), the Yupana represents numbers horizontally, although the orders are represented vertically (<htcite4>).

We have already seen different ways of representing quantities, although all of them represent exactly the same idea of numerical families (orders).

Soroban (算盤)Suanpan (算盤)

Figure 1: Abacus. On the left, we have a Soroban (算盤) by Shigetoshi Oyabu. Maintained by Tohoku University Main Library (). On the right, we have a Suanpan (算盤). © Trustees of the British Museum ()

Reflection 1: It must be for...

Observing Figure 1, how do you think numbers are represented on the abacuses (Soroban and Suanpan (算盤))?

### Between the two

In general terms, we can understand that both the Soroban and the Suanpan (算盤) have characteristics of both number systems we have studied so far.

#### In common with indigenous systems

When representing a number on any version of the abacus, we always move the markers toward the bar. When no marker has been moved, we have the value 0, and we accumulate values up to the limit of 9 (<htcite0>).

#### In common with the Indo-Arabic system

Since each column of the abacuses represents only one number in the range of 0 to 9, to deal with higher orders it is necessary to use the other columns to represent the other orders, as we will see in the following.

### Representing on the Soroban (算盤)

We will continue our studies with abacuses, starting with the Soroban (算盤), as it was the first abacus we studied, and also because it does not allow numbers greater than nine to be represented in a single column (<htcite0>).

#### How to represent?

The steps for representing numbers between 0 and 9 have already been presented earlier (<htcite1>), so we will not go into details. We will only reinforce that each lower bead (earth) has a value of 1, while the upper bead (heaven) is worth 5.

#### From smallest to largest

The first step to writing a number that goes beyond 0 and 9 is to choose where the decimal marker (⚫) will be placed on the abacus. By keeping it in the current position, the columns of our abacus will respectively represent:

Column 9Column 8Column 7Column 6Column 5Column 4Column 3Column 2Column 1
Hundred Million Ten Million Million Hundred Thousand Ten Thousand Thousand Hundred Ten Unit
Table 1: Numerical orders (families) represented on the Soroban when the decimal marker is in the first column on the right.

From **Table 1**, we can conclude that the position of the decimal marker (⚫) will indicate where the units are (<htcite0>). From that marker onward, numbers increase to the left. Let's practice to reinforce what we have learned.

Value: 0
⚠️There is/are column(s) with values greater than 9! Each digit must remain between 0 and 9.
🧮 Abacus:
⚫ Decimal marker

Practice 1: Growing...
In this practice, we will work with all columns and keep the decimal marker (⚫) in the rightmost position.

1. Click the “Soroban (1x4)” button.
2. Move one lower bead toward the bar. Notice that the number 1 will be displayed.
3. Now, go to the second column, the tens column (Table 1), and move two lower beads. This will show the value 21.
4. Next, go to the following column, the hundreds column, and move three lower beads. This will give the number 321.
5. Continuing, represent the value 4 in the thousands column (the fourth column), thus obtaining the number 4,321.
6. Our next action will be to place the number 5 in the ten-thousands column. Therefore, we will move only the upper bead, obtaining the value 54,321.
7. Proceed representing the numbers to the left until you complete the value 987,654,321.

### Representing on the Suanpan (算盤)

To represent numbers on the Suanpan (算盤), we follow the same steps as on the Soroban (算盤). This means we move the lower beads (earth) to represent numbers one by one, while we move the upper beads (heaven) to represent the number 5.

When we want to represent higher orders, we also use the numbers to the left of our decimal marker (⚫).

However, because the Suanpan (算盤) has more beads than the Soroban (算盤), we need, in some cases, to make additional movements.

Let's practice to make this clearer.

Practice 2: Changing order on the Suanpan
In this practice, we will see how the Suanpan indicates to the learner that we must represent another number further to the left.

1. Click the “Suanpan (2x5)” button..

2. Move five lower beads toward the bar.
3.
3. Now move a single upper bead. You will see an alert message appear: “There is/are column(s) with values greater than 9! Each digit must remain between 0 and 9.”.

Whenever any number between 10 and 15 is represented in a column of the Suanpan, we must move the “excess” (10) to the next column, keeping the remaining values. Let's continue with the practice to reinforce this.

1. Move the upper bead back to its original position.

2. Move all the lower beads back to their original position.

3. Now, go to the tens column, the second from right to left, and place 1. You will see that the displayed value will be 10.

In this practice with the Suanpan (算盤), we observed how the History Tracers version of the Suanpan (算盤) alerts about values greater than 9 in each numerical order (family) and how to proceed to adjust the values and obtain the expected results.

This characteristic of the Suanpan (算盤) helps us reinforce that we cannot represent numbers other than 0 to 9 in each numerical order (family).

We suggest you repeat **Practice 1**, this time with the Suanpan (算盤), to confirm that both work in the same way.

### Soroban and Suanpan (算盤) in nature

In this text, we saw different abacuses that share the same idea of representing quantities.

In the same way, we could create our own abacuses even in a desert region, such as the sacred city of Caral (**Figure 1**) (<htcite0>).
Figure 2: Pyramid H1 at the Caral archaeological site (). Photo taken on , during a visit to the site.
For example, we could draw a line in the sand that would represent the bar next to which we will place small rocks, which will be our markers.

Practice 3: Do it yourself
Let's build our own abacus with only 3 columns (Table 2).

1. Take a sheet of paper and draw a straight line on it (in Table 2, it is represented by a white line).

2. Now get the markers. You can make them by cutting pieces of paper and rolling them up. Or you can use beans. Or any object that can be moved vertically and placed side by side horizontally.

3. Finally, arrange them as in the abacus of Table 2.

Manual abacus with 3 columns
⚫⚫⚫
   
 
   
⚫⚫⚫
⚫⚫⚫
⚫⚫⚫
⚫⚫⚫
⚫⚫⚫
Table 2: Example of a manual abacus.

### Conclusion

In this text, we learned how to use the Soroban and Suanpan (算盤) abacuses to represent larger numerical orders (families).

Furthermore, with the knowledge acquired while working with these tools, we saw that it is possible to build them using materials that can be found in different places.

exercise_v2.question: In both abacuses, Soroban and Suanpan (算盤), are numerical orders represented vertically?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: Although we represent counting vertically, when we talk about numerical orders (units, tens, hundreds...), they are represented horizontally.

exercise_v2.question: Does the marker on the horizontal bar represent the position of the largest order we observe?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: The marker on the horizontal bar shows us the place of the smallest order, that is, the unit.
